metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'reconcile' in AC3 with a specific disposition: either 'codify rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high in infra/terraform/monitoring.tf and import into state' OR 'delete the alarm from AWS and document why it is not needed', so the executor has a single unambiguous action.
findings.finding: AC3 uses the vague verb 'reconcile' without specifying the intended disposition of the orphan alarm (import into Terraform state, codify in HCL, or delete), deferring a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` (GitHub Actions OIDC) and a repo-scoped IAM role already exist in account 896586841071, and if not, add their creation (or a reference to the ticket/PR that creates them) as an explicit prerequisite or sub-task of AC2.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as the authentication mechanism for the drift-detection CI, but no such OIDC provider or role exists anywhere in the repo's IaC (no aws_iam_openid_connect_provider, no token.actions.githubusercontent.com trust policy, no matching IAM role in any .tf file). The plan neither states it already exists nor specifies creating it, leaving the CI's auth prerequisite as an unbacked implicit assumption.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-task or prerequisite to AC2: 'Create aws_iam_openid_connect_provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com and an aws_iam_role with a repo-scoped (repo:navapbc/rebar:*) trust policy in infra/terraform/ (or infra/bootstrap/) before wiring the CI workflow.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `-detailed-exitcode` (or equivalent, e.g. `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` + check `$? -eq 2`) in AC2 so the CI step exits non-zero when there are unapplied changes.
findings.finding: `terraform plan` exits with code 0 even when there are pending changes to apply — the CI job as described would PASS on drift, not fail, unless `-detailed-exitcode` (exit 2 on diff) is specified. The AC says 'fails on un-applied drift' but the mechanism as written does not achieve that behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan... and fails on un-applied drift'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC2 to read: '...runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` on changes under infra/terraform/ and fails (exit 2) when there are un-applied changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the intended reconciliation path for AC3: either 'import into HCL/state and document the metric source' (keeping coverage) or 'delete from AWS after confirming it is not intentional coverage' — do not leave both options open.
findings.finding: AC3 ('reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high') specifies no chosen mechanism — import into terraform state (keeping the alarm) vs. delete from AWS (removing alerting coverage). These have opposite operational outcomes and the correct choice depends on whether the alarm is intentional coverage, yet the plan leaves the choice entirely open.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high (live but absent from HCL/state)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or add HCL for the GitHub OIDC provider (`aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) and the GitHub Actions IAM role with a scoped trust policy in `infra/terraform/iam.tf` (or a new `iam_cicd.tf`); reference the HCL resource in AC2 rather than asserting the role exists.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as though it already exists, but no aws_iam_openid_connect_provider resource and no GitHub Actions IAM role appear anywhere in infra/terraform/. This is an unverified capability assertion at a security/auth integration bright line: the OIDC provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com must be registered in the account before any CI workflow can assume a role via it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource for `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com` and an `aws_iam_role` with a condition-scoped assume-role policy (e.g. `repo:navapbc/rebar:*`) to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` or a new `infra/terraform/iam_cicd.tf`. Cite these resources explicitly in AC2 so the plan is self-contained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the GitHub OIDC provider (`token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) and its associated IAM role are new HCL resources to be added to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` (or a new file), document the required IAM permissions (minimum: S3 state-bucket read + lockfile write, plus read-only permissions for all resources `terraform plan` queries), and note that the OIDC thumbprint must be present — otherwise the CI auth precondition is unmet and AC2 is unimplementable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as a ready-to-use auth precondition for the drift-detection CI workflow, but no OIDC provider or OIDC-capable IAM role exists anywhere in infra/terraform/ (zero matches for 'aws_iam_openid_connect_provider', 'token.actions.githubusercontent', 'oidc'). The plan does not specify whether this is a new HCL resource to add, a manually pre-created role, what IAM permissions it requires (state-bucket read, lockfile write, CloudWatch/EC2/SNS read for plan), or how the OIDC thumbprint and audience are configured. Without this role the CI workflow cannot authenticate to AWS at all.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite to AC2: 'Add `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` and an `aws_iam_role` (e.g. `rebar-github-actions-terraform-plan`) with a scoped read-only policy (S3 state read, all resources `terraform plan` reads) to `infra/terraform/iam.tf`. The CI workflow assumes this role via `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4` with `role-to-assume`. Document the role ARN as a repo variable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC2 to specify `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` (exits 2 on planned changes) so the CI step can fail on drift; a plain `terraform plan` exits 0 on both clean and drifted states and will silently pass.
findings.finding: `terraform plan` exits 0 both when there are NO changes and when there ARE planned changes (drift); it only exits non-zero (1) on hard errors. The AC states the CI 'fails on un-applied drift', but a naive `terraform plan` step in CI will always pass when terraform itself succeeds — even when drift is detected. The correct invocation requires `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode`, which exits 2 when changes are present, allowing CI to fail on drift.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'fails on un-applied drift'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2 wording to: 'runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` … and fails (exit 2) on un-applied drift'. In the workflow step: `run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan` with `continue-on-error: false`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the GitHub Actions IAM role's trust-policy `Condition` (at minimum `StringLike` on `token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub` scoped to `repo:navapbc/rebar:*`) and its permission set (scoped to S3 `GetObject`/`ListBucket` on the state bucket and AWS API describe-only actions for resources under `infra/terraform/`) in the plan or the IaC PR.
findings.finding: AC2 introduces a GitHub OIDC → AWS IAM trust relationship (an external lower-trust actor gaining AWS credentials) but does not declare the OIDC sub-claim conditions that restrict which GitHub repo/branch/workflow/environment can assume the role, nor the IAM permissions (actions + resources) the role will hold.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new IaC for the CI role, add a trust-policy condition such as: `StringEquals: {"token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:navapbc/rebar:ref:refs/heads/main"}` (or a `StringLike` wildcard covering only PR branches in that repo). Grant only the minimum AWS permissions `terraform plan` requires: `s3:GetObject`, `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::rebar-tfstate-896586841071`, plus a tightly scoped `iam:Get*`, `ec2:Describe*`, `cloudwatch:Describe*`, `sns:Get*`, `ssm:DescribeParameters` set. State the chosen permission boundary explicitly in the plan's AC2 text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC3 with a specific, observable end-state: either 'rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high is codified in infra/terraform/monitoring.tf and present in terraform state' OR 'rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high is deleted from AWS and a decision record is added to infra/runbooks/' — not both.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high') leaves the ownership and retirement path undefined — 'reconcile' could mean import-into-state, add HCL, or delete — so neither a human nor an automated agent can determine when the AC is satisfied or how to avoid leaking the live resource.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2 to require a scheduled (e.g., daily cron) or post-merge `terraform plan` run on the main branch, not only on PRs, to catch already-committed-but-unapplied drift.
findings.finding: The drift-detection CI (AC2) is scoped to 'changes under infra/terraform/' (i.e., PR-triggered), which does not catch the scenario it was designed to prevent: HCL already committed to main but not applied. A scheduled or post-merge plan run is needed to surface existing drift.
findings.location: AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second GitHub Actions workflow triggered by `schedule: cron` (e.g., daily) that runs `terraform plan` against the main branch state and fails/notifies on any diff, in addition to the PR-time check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC2 sub-criterion requiring a demonstrated failure path: 'CI must exit non-zero when a synthetic unapplied resource is present in infra/terraform/; a test branch proving this behavior is required before the workflow is merged.'
findings.finding: AC2 (drift-detection CI) has no stated test that the CI pipeline actually fails when genuine drift exists; the AC is satisfiable by a pipeline that only runs 'terraform plan' on already-applied state, meaning the failure path (the whole point of the CI) is never exercised — a proxy-validation gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-time PR to infra/terraform/monitoring.tf with a dummy null_resource (never applied) and confirm the new CI workflow exits non-zero on that branch before merging the CI itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete validation mechanism to each AC: for AC1, specify a 'trigger a test CloudWatch alarm and verify email/Slack receipt' step; for AC2, specify a 'commit a synthetic drift change and confirm CI exits non-zero' acceptance check.
findings.finding: No concrete end-to-end validation mechanism is specified for either the SNS subscription fix or the drift-detection CI; the ACs state desired system state but not how a human or automated check would confirm the alerting chain actually works for a stakeholder.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: AC1 addendum: 'Validated by: manually publishing a test SNS message and confirming receipt at the subscriber.' AC2 addendum: 'Validated by: a CI run on a branch with a dummy resource added to monitoring.tf that has never been applied — workflow must exit non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command or verification step for each AC: (AC1) publish a test message (`aws sns publish`) and confirm receipt at the destination; (AC2) run the drift-detection workflow against a branch with a known HCL change and assert the plan exits non-zero; (AC3) run `terraform plan` and assert zero diff after reconciliation.
findings.finding: No proving commands or verification steps are described for any of the three ACs; the plan has no steps section, leaving every criterion without a concrete check that would produce evidence of success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting into at least two child tickets: one for the SNS subscription fix + orphan alarm reconciliation (both are monitoring.tf / human-action concerns), and one for the terraform drift CI (new pipeline project) — each is independently releasable and testable.
findings.finding: The plan bundles three independently releasable and independently valuable concerns — (1) SNS subscription remediation (human inbox action), (2) terraform drift CI (new infra pipeline project), and (3) orphan alarm reconciliation — each with a distinct actor/reason-to-change, violating single-concern.
findings.location: Scope section; Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC2, AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC2: specify that the plan creates an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` AND constrains the role's permission policy to plan-only (state read + relevant `Describe*` actions, no `Create/Delete/Update`).
findings.finding: AC2 names 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as the auth mechanism but does not specify: (a) who/what creates the prerequisite aws_iam_openid_connect_provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com (absent from all infra/terraform/*.tf), and (b) what permission scope the role carries — plan-only (read + state) vs. apply-level write. Without a read-only permission boundary, a CI compromise yields arbitrary AWS resource mutation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the minimum IAM permission set for the GitHub OIDC CI role in AC2 (or reference a new `infra/terraform/iam_ci.tf`): at minimum S3:GetObject/ListBucket on `rebar-tfstate-896586841071`, plus read-only describe/list on EC2, SNS, CloudWatch, SSM, IAM, and DLM in `us-east-1` — no write permissions.
findings.finding: AC2 introduces a new GitHub OIDC IAM role in account 896586841071 for the drift-detection CI, but the plan specifies no permission scope for that role. A terraform plan runner needs S3 read on the state bucket plus describe/list on every managed resource class (EC2, SNS, CloudWatch, SSM, IAM, DLM). If the implementer defaults to a broad managed policy (e.g., ReadOnlyAccess or PowerUserAccess) or — worse — re-uses the existing operator role, the principle of least privilege is violated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a named `iam_ci.tf` that creates the OIDC provider (if not present) and an `aws_iam_role` with a trust policy scoped to `repo:navapbc/rebar:ref:refs/heads/*` and a least-privilege inline policy granting only the describe/list/get actions required by `terraform plan` against the specific resource ARNs managed in this workspace.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC3, make the reconciliation approach an explicit, justified choice: state whether the orphan alarm should be imported into HCL (with the required HCL block shown or referenced) or deleted (with justification), and document the undo path for deletion (e.g., re-apply the HCL block).
findings.finding: AC3 'reconcile' the orphan alarm is ambiguous between import-into-state and deletion; deletion is irreversible (the live alarm would be permanently removed) but the plan does not identify this as an explicit, justified destructive choice or provide a rollback path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope bullet and brief description in 'What' for the orphan alarm reconciliation (AC3), clarifying whether it should be imported into HCL/state or deleted, and update 'Two follow-ups' to reflect three items.
findings.finding: The 'What' section declares exactly 'Two follow-ups' and uses 'Neither is autonomously completable' (implying exactly two items), but the plan defines three Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (reconcile orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high) has no counterpart in 'What' or 'Scope', making it an orphan criterion with no described work backing it.
findings.location: What section vs Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In 'What', change 'Two follow-ups' to 'Three follow-ups' and add a third bullet. In 'Scope', add: 'The live alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high exists in AWS but is absent from infra/terraform/monitoring.tf and Terraform state; it must be either imported into HCL (if intentional) or removed (if orphaned).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to AC2 explaining why plan-only drift detection (not automated apply) was chosen — e.g. 'applies remain intentionally manual per provision-restore.md to preserve human review of every infrastructure change.'
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing drift-detection-only (terraform plan, no apply) over a plan+apply CI approach. The existing runbook posture requires manual applies, but this is not articulated as a deliberate design choice in the plan — leaving implementers without guidance on whether to add an apply gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or AC2: 'CI runs plan-only (no automated apply) to preserve the deliberate manual-apply gate documented in infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the target .tf file (e.g. `infra/terraform/iam.tf` or a new `infra/terraform/iam_ci.tf`) for the new `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` and GitHub OIDC IAM role required by AC2.
findings.finding: AC2 requires a new aws_iam_openid_connect_provider resource and a repo-scoped IAM role for GitHub Actions→AWS trust, but the plan names no target .tf file for these IAM resources. infra/terraform/iam.tf is the existing owner of all IAM resources in the module, yet it is not listed as an edit target. ADR-0026 explicitly records the current architecture has 'no GitHub→AWS trust surface (no OIDC / ssm:SendCommand grant)' — making iam.tf (or a new iam_ci.tf) a mandatory but unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan … GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Scope or AC2: 'Add aws_iam_openid_connect_provider + a repo-scoped aws_iam_role to infra/terraform/iam.tf (or a dedicated infra/terraform/iam_ci.tf) following the single-owner contract documented in monitoring.tf.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If the AWS Chatbot/Slack path is a realistic choice for AC1, add a precondition noting that a Slack workspace must first be authorized in the AWS console (AWS Chatbot → Configure new client) before `terraform apply` can create the channel configuration; alternatively, prefer the HTTPS or SQS+Lambda alternatives which have no out-of-band OAuth step.
findings.finding: AC1 lists 'AWS Chatbot/Slack' as a confirmation-free SNS sink alternative, but AWS Chatbot's Slack integration requires a one-time OAuth authorization flow completed by a Slack workspace admin in the AWS console — this cannot be fully automated via Terraform alone and has no stated auth/setup precondition in the plan. If the Chatbot/Slack path is chosen, the plan gives no guidance that this console step must precede the `terraform apply`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'replace it with a confirmation-free sink (AWS Chatbot/Slack, HTTPS, or SQS+Lambda)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the preferred implementation path for AC1 (e.g., 'prefer email confirmation if subscription is still pending; otherwise replace with SQS+Lambda') so the executor does not need to make an unguided design choice.
findings.finding: AC1 presents two alternative implementations ('confirm the pending email OR replace it with a confirmation-free sink') with no stated preference, decision criterion, or fallback trigger, leaving the executor to make an unguided design choice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a decision rule: 'First check subscription status via `aws sns list-subscriptions-by-topic`. If status is still PendingConfirmation, re-send the confirmation email and document the confirmed ARN. If the subscription has auto-deleted, replace it with an HTTPS endpoint (SQS+Lambda in infra/terraform/monitoring.tf) as the preferred confirmation-free sink.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit expand-contract sequence for AC1's replacement path: provision and validate the new sink before removing the old subscription, and document the rollback step (re-add the email subscription) if the new sink fails.
findings.finding: AC1's 'OR replace it with a confirmation-free sink' path is a destructive substitution (remove existing SNS subscription, add new) with no expand-contract sequencing or rollback plan stated, so alerting could go dark if the new sink fails to work as expected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, add: 'If replacing the subscription, use an expand-contract approach: (1) add the new sink in monitoring.tf and apply, (2) verify end-to-end delivery via a test SNS publish, (3) then remove the PendingConfirmation subscription. Rollback: re-add the email subscription resource and re-apply.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the target file path for the new drift-detection GitHub Actions workflow (e.g. `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml`) in AC2 or the Scope section.
findings.finding: AC2 requires a new GitHub Actions workflow file for drift-detection CI, but no target file path under .github/workflows/ is named. All existing workflows are visible in .github/workflows/ and no terraform-drift or plan-check workflow exists there; the new file is a net-new artifact (CREATION) whose path is unspecified in the plan.
findings.location: AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the target .tf file (or runbook/doc file if the resolution is to leave it unmanaged) for reconciling the rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high orphan alarm in AC3.
findings.finding: AC3 says 'Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high (live but absent from HCL/state)' but names no target .tf file where the alarm would be declared if the chosen resolution is to import it into state and codify it. infra/terraform/ has per-concern monitoring files (monitoring.tf, monitoring_1fa8.tf, monitoring_s4b.tf, etc.) and no file is identified as the home for this orphan.
findings.location: AC3: 'Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the terraform drift-detection CI decision (pattern chosen, OIDC role rationale, failure policy, and why alternatives like Atlantis were not used).
findings.finding: The plan introduces the project's first terraform CI (a novel architectural/process decision explicitly noted: 'No terraform CI exists') but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the pattern, the chosen OIDC role, or the failure policy — leaving future maintainers without rationale.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/'
findings.suggested_fix: Create infra/docs/adr-terraform-drift-ci.md (or append to an existing ADR file) covering: (1) decision to use GitHub Actions + OIDC over other options, (2) the OIDC role ARN and trust scope, (3) the policy when CI is unavailable (non-blocking vs blocking), and (4) link from infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a timeout and failure-mode policy for the drift-detection CI job (e.g., job-level timeout, whether AWS API errors are reported as a warning vs. hard failure, and an alert/notification path when the job fails for non-drift reasons).
findings.finding: The drift-detection CI invokes `terraform plan` against live AWS as an external dependency, but the plan does not specify a timeout, retry strategy, or graceful-degradation policy for when AWS APIs are unavailable — risking false-positive CI failures that block PRs unrelated to actual drift.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ … and fails on un-applied drift'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CI workflow definition, set a `timeout-minutes` on the terraform plan step, add a step that distinguishes exit code 1 (error) from exit code 2 (diff detected), and emit a GitHub Actions warning annotation or Slack notification on exit code 1 so operators can triage AWS-side failures separately from genuine drift.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make AC1's delivery verifiable in-session by adding a concrete post-confirmation check: after whichever sink is chosen, publish a test SNS message (`aws sns publish --topic-arn <arn> --message 'test'`) and assert the message arrives at the destination (email inbox receipt screenshot, Slack message, or SQS message count).
findings.finding: AC1's primary path ('confirm the pending email') requires a human inbox action that cannot be completed in-session by an agent, yet no fallback or in-session-completable proxy check is described to validate delivery end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit retirement/cleanup step for the existing PendingConfirmation subscription (delete it before or after replacement) and enumerate/verify that the CloudWatch alarm's SNS action remains wired correctly after any sink change.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens to the existing PendingConfirmation SNS subscription when it is confirmed OR replaced: no retirement/cleanup path is described, and no enumeration of other consumers of the SNS topic (e.g., the CloudWatch alarm→SNS wire) is provided to ensure they remain tolerant of the transition.
findings.location: AC1 / Scope – SNS subscription section
findings.suggested_fix: In infra/terraform/monitoring.tf, set the SNS subscription resource to the replacement sink and ensure no dangling aws_sns_topic_subscription resource exists for the pending email endpoint; add a note to the runbook to manually delete the PendingConfirmation subscription from the console before apply if Terraform does not own it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document how state-lock concurrency between the new CI plan job and existing manual applies is handled (e.g., lock timeout, CI retry, or a policy that CI never runs during apply windows).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a CI job that runs `terraform plan` against shared Terraform state but does not address state-locking concurrency: a CI plan run racing with a manual `terraform apply` (the current workflow per infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md) could produce misleading plan output or corrupt the lock.
findings.location: AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC1, demote 'SQS+Lambda' and 'AWS Chatbot/Slack' from peer options to last-resort footnotes (or remove them), and promote the minimal fallback path — an HTTPS endpoint or, simplest of all, re-confirming the existing email — as the preferred alternative when the inbox click is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan lists 'SQS+Lambda' as a peer alternative to email confirmation for the SNS alert sink. Lambda functions and SQS queues have zero existing call-sites in the codebase (grep confirms no aws_lambda_function, no aws_sqs_queue anywhere under infra/terraform/). Introducing them for a single SNS subscription would violate Rule-of-Three and YAGNI — it is disproportionate engineering for a one-off alerting sink that the primary path (email confirmation) already solves.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '…replace it with a confirmation-free sink (AWS Chatbot/Slack, HTTPS, or SQS+Lambda)…'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC1 as: 'Confirm the pending email to joeoakhart@navapbc.com. If the subscription has auto-deleted (>72h), re-apply to recreate it and confirm. Only if a confirmation-free sink is strictly required, prefer an HTTPS endpoint (aws_sns_topic_subscription protocol="https") — avoid introducing net-new service dependencies (Lambda/SQS/Chatbot) that have no existing footprint in the codebase.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC2: the CI workflow must pin Terraform >= 1.10 (matching `required_version` in versions.tf) to preserve S3-native state locking during concurrent plan runs.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a Terraform CLI version requirement for the CI runner. The existing backend uses `use_lockfile = true` (versions.tf:18), and versions.tf explicitly warns that Terraform < 1.10 silently ignores this flag and runs with NO state locking. A CI runner pinned below 1.10 could corrupt the remote state if a plan runs concurrently with a human apply.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; also infra/terraform/versions.tf lines 7-10
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3` step in the workflow with `terraform_version: '~> 1.10'` or specify the version constraint in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how AC3 will be resolved: either (a) `terraform import aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.data_disk_high rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high` plus a corresponding HCL block in monitoring.tf, or (b) `aws cloudwatch delete-alarms --alarm-names rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high` with a rationale for why the alarm is safe to remove.
findings.finding: AC3 directs the implementer to 'reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high (live but absent from HCL/state)' without specifying the reconciliation method — import it into Terraform state (and add HCL) or delete it out-of-band. Until the orphan is resolved by one of these methods, the drift-detection CI added in AC2 will report it as unknown/extra-state drift on its first run, creating an immediately broken CI gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high (live but absent from HCL/state)'
findings.suggested_fix: If the data-disk metric is still needed, add `resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "data_disk_high"` to monitoring.tf and run `terraform import` so it enters state; if it is superseded by an existing alarm, delete it out-of-band and document the decision in a comment in monitoring.tf.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
